result:
The first thing that comes to me is the quiet. Not a silence, but a quiet. The low hum of the laptop, the distant sigh of a refrigerator, the faint, almost-imagined tinnitus ringing in my own ears—a lifelong companion. It’s in this quiet that the vast, terrifying, and beautiful landscape of “whatever” opens up. One thousand words is not a constraint; it’s a meadow. You can run in any direction.

I think of the weight of history in my hand yesterday, holding a flint arrowhead found in a Scottish field. It was cold, smoother than glass, fitted perfectly into the dip of my thumb. Some person, millennia ago, sat and knapped that stone. They blew the dust away, tested the edge, maybe nicked their own thumb and cursed in a language that has evaporated from the earth. They attached it to a shaft with sinew and pitch, hoping it would feed their family or protect them. And then it was lost. For thousands of years, it slept in the peat, while empires rose and fell, while Shakespeare wrote and rockets pierced the moon. Now it sits on my desk, a paperweight for unpaid bills. The absolute, unbridgeable distance between that person and me is somehow contained in that one inch of stone. We are connected by the fact of touch, separated by an ocean of time. I put it down carefully, as if it might wake up.

This makes me think of time, not as a line, but as a kind of soup. Everything is floating in it—the arrowhead, the feeling I had when I was seven and saw a double rainbow, the unsent angry letter, the memory of my grandmother’s perfume, the yet-to-exist moment of my last breath. We are just straining our ladle through it, pulling out a chronological sequence of “nows,” but the rest of it is all still there, swirling. Maybe deja vu is just a moment when your ladle bumps against a chunk of the future, or the past, already floating in your broth.

The window shows a grey afternoon. A squirrel executes a terrifying, death-defying leap between two branches, for no reason I can discern other than the sheer joy of motion. It doesn’t worry about mortgages or climate reports or the strange new ache in its knee. It just is. There’s a lesson there, I’m sure, but I’m too human to fully learn it. We are the worrying animal. We are the creature that builds calendars and then feels guilty for not filling the squares efficiently enough.

I remember a friend once said, “You have to love the *dailiness* of life.” The making of the tea, the scrubbing of the plate, the tying of the shoelace. Not as a spiritual practice, but as the actual fabric. The grand moments are the embroidery, but the cloth is the daily. The thread is made of quiet mornings, of waiting for the toast to pop, of noticing the way the light slants through a dusty window at 4 p.m. in November. That specific, golden, melancholy light that seems to hold the memory of every other autumn afternoon you’ve ever lived.

What are we, any of us, but collections of these sensed fragments? I am the smell of rain on hot asphalt. I am the opening chord of “A Hard Day’s Night.” I am the weight of a sleeping cat on my legs. I am the taste of a specific sour apple candy I haven’t had since 1989. I am the sharp, metallic fear of a missed step in the dark. We are not grand narratives; we are mosaics made of a million insignificant, sensory tiles.

And yet, we try to make stories. We can’t help it. We look at the arrowhead and invent a hunter. We look at the stars and invent constellations. We look at the chaos of our lives and try to craft a plot with meaning, with cause and effect, with a character arc. Maybe that’s our real superpower: not tool-making, but story-making. It’s how we survive the soup of time. We carve out a little narrative raft and float on it.

I think about the people I love. How love itself is not a constant emotion, but a deep, underlying knowing. It’s the background process running on the computer of your soul. Sometimes it pops up a notification—a surge of warmth, a pang of worry—but most of the time, it’s just there, the operating system. You don’t think about your breath until you’re underwater; you don’t think about your love until it is threatened.

The quiet is back. The hum remains. A thousand words is almost up, and I haven’t said anything about politics, or the great crises of the age. But perhaps that’s the point of “whatever.” In the face of the vast, churning world, there is also this: a single consciousness, in a quiet room, trying to connect a flint arrowhead to a squirrel’s leap to the color of the afternoon light. Trying to feel the thread that runs through it all.

Maybe the thread is just attention. The paying of attention. To the artifact, to the animal, to the light, to the breath. To the mysterious, brief, and bewildering fact of being here at all, with a ladle in your hand, standing over the simmering soup of everything.

The last words approach. I find I want to end not with a conclusion, but with an invitation. Look away from this. Look at what’s beside you. The pen on the desk. The crack in the ceiling. The sound of your own breath. There is a universe there. In the *whatever* that is right before you. In the daily. In the quiet. In the thread you are spinning, right now, just by noticing.

And it’s enough.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
